title: System Design Roadmap
description: Master system design from fundamentals to designing large-scale distributed systems for interviews and real-world applications.

topics.title: What is System Design?
topics.description: System design is the process of defining the architecture, components, modules, interfaces, and data for a system to satisfy specified requirements. It involves making high-level decisions about how different parts of a system interact.

interviewQuestions.q: What is system design and why is it important?
interviewQuestions.a: System design is the process of defining the architecture, modules, interfaces, and data flow of a system. It is important because it ensures the system can handle scale, maintain reliability, and meet performance requirements. Good system design reduces costs, improves maintainability, and enables teams to build systems that grow gracefully.

interviewQuestions.q: What are the key steps in approaching a system design problem?
interviewQuestions.a: 1) Clarify requirements and constraints, 2) Estimate scale (users, data, requests), 3) Define the high-level architecture, 4) Design core components in detail, 5) Identify bottlenecks and trade-offs, 6) Address scalability, reliability, and monitoring.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the difference between high-level design and low-level design?
interviewQuestions.a: High-level design (HLD) focuses on the overall system architecture — components, their interactions, data flow, and technology choices. Low-level design (LLD) dives into specific component implementation — class diagrams, database schemas, API contracts, and algorithms used within each module.

tips: Always start by clarifying functional and non-functional requirements before jumping into architecture.
Practice drawing system diagrams — interviewers want to see clear, structured thinking.

topics.title: How to Approach System Design
topics.description: A structured approach to system design involves understanding requirements, estimating scale, designing high-level architecture, diving into component details, and addressing bottlenecks.

interviewQuestions.q: Walk me through your approach to a system design interview.
interviewQuestions.a: I follow a structured framework: 1) Requirements gathering (5 min) — clarify functional/non-functional needs, 2) Back-of-envelope estimation (5 min) — calculate QPS, storage, bandwidth, 3) High-level design (10 min) — draw the main components and data flow, 4) Detailed design (15 min) — dive deep into 2-3 critical components, 5) Bottlenecks and trade-offs (5 min) — discuss scaling, caching, monitoring.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you handle ambiguous requirements in system design?
interviewQuestions.a: I ask clarifying questions to narrow scope: Who are the users? What is the expected scale? What are the most critical features? What are acceptable latency/availability targets? I also state assumptions explicitly so the interviewer can correct them early.

interviewQuestions.q: What are back-of-the-envelope estimations and why are they useful?
interviewQuestions.a: They are rough calculations to estimate system scale — daily active users, queries per second, storage needs, and bandwidth. They help determine if a single server suffices or if distributed architecture is needed, and guide decisions on database choice, caching strategy, and infrastructure sizing.

tips: Use the RESHADED framework: Requirements, Estimation, Storage schema, High-level design, API design, Detailed design, Evaluation, Deployment.
Never jump straight to solutions — spend the first 5 minutes asking clarifying questions.

topics.title: Performance vs Scalability
topics.description: A service is scalable if it results in increased performance proportional to resources added. Performance is about how fast a single request is served, while scalability is about serving more requests as load increases.

resources.title: Performance vs Scalability - System Design Primer

interviewQuestions.q: What is the difference between performance and scalability?
interviewQuestions.a: Performance refers to how quickly a system responds to a single request (latency, throughput for one user). Scalability refers to the system's ability to handle growing amounts of work by adding resources. A system can be performant but not scalable (fast for one user, breaks at 1000 users), or scalable but not performant (handles millions of users but each request is slow).

interviewQuestions.q: How do you identify performance bottlenecks in a system?
interviewQuestions.a: Use profiling tools, monitoring (APM), load testing, and distributed tracing. Look at database query times, CPU/memory utilization, network latency, and I/O wait times. Common bottlenecks include slow database queries, insufficient caching, synchronous blocking calls, and network round trips.

interviewQuestions.q: How does adding more resources improve scalability?
interviewQuestions.a: Vertical scaling adds CPU/RAM to existing servers for immediate performance gains but has limits. Horizontal scaling adds more servers to distribute load, requiring load balancers and stateless design. The choice depends on your bottleneck — CPU-bound tasks benefit from vertical scaling, while I/O-bound and stateless workloads scale better horizontally.

tips: If you have a performance problem, your system is slow for a single user. If you have a scalability problem, your system is fast for one user but slow under load.
Always measure before optimizing — premature optimization is the root of all evil.

topics.title: Latency vs Throughput
topics.description: Latency is the time to perform some action or produce a result. Throughput is the number of such actions or results per unit of time. Generally, you should aim for maximum throughput with acceptable latency.

resources.title: Latency vs Throughput - System Design Primer
Latency Numbers Every Programmer Should Know

interviewQuestions.q: What is the difference between latency and throughput?
interviewQuestions.a: Latency is the time taken for a single operation (e.g., 50ms for a database read). Throughput is the number of operations completed per unit time (e.g., 1000 requests/second). They are related but distinct — you can have low latency with low throughput (fast but sequential), or high throughput with high latency (many concurrent slow operations).

interviewQuestions.q: What are typical latency numbers a programmer should know?
interviewQuestions.a: L1 cache: ~0.5ns, L2 cache: ~7ns, RAM: ~100ns, SSD random read: ~150μs, HDD seek: ~10ms, same datacenter round trip: ~0.5ms, cross-continent round trip: ~150ms. These numbers help estimate system performance and identify where bottlenecks will occur.

interviewQuestions.q: How can you optimize for both low latency and high throughput?
interviewQuestions.a: Use caching to reduce latency for repeated operations, asynchronous processing to improve throughput, connection pooling to reduce overhead, CDNs to reduce network latency, batch processing to improve throughput, and efficient serialization formats. Sometimes you must trade off one for the other — batch processing improves throughput but increases per-item latency.

tips: Memorize the latency numbers chart — it comes up frequently in interviews and helps you make quick architecture decisions.
Aim for maximal throughput with acceptable latency as your design goal.

topics.title: Availability vs Consistency
topics.description: In distributed systems, you cannot have both perfect availability and perfect consistency simultaneously (per CAP theorem). Understanding this trade-off is crucial for making architecture decisions.

interviewQuestions.q: Explain the trade-off between availability and consistency.
interviewQuestions.a: In a distributed system during a network partition, you must choose: either remain available (serve potentially stale data) or remain consistent (reject requests until all nodes agree). CP systems like HBase prioritize consistency — they may become unavailable during partitions. AP systems like Cassandra prioritize availability — they serve requests but may return stale data.

interviewQuestions.q: When would you choose availability over consistency?
interviewQuestions.a: For systems where stale data is acceptable temporarily — social media feeds, product catalogs, analytics dashboards, DNS. Users can tolerate seeing slightly outdated information. E-commerce product listings, news feeds, and content delivery are classic AP use cases.

interviewQuestions.q: When would you choose consistency over availability?
interviewQuestions.a: For systems where incorrect data causes serious problems — financial transactions, inventory management, booking systems, authentication. A bank transfer must be consistent — you cannot show different balances on different nodes. Booking systems must avoid double-booking the same resource.

tips: In interviews, always ask whether the system needs strong consistency or if eventual consistency is acceptable — this drives major architecture decisions.
Most real-world systems use eventual consistency with compensation mechanisms rather than strict CP or AP.

topics.title: CAP Theorem
topics.description: The CAP theorem states that a distributed data store can provide at most two of three guarantees: Consistency (every read receives the most recent write), Availability (every request receives a response), and Partition Tolerance (the system continues to operate despite network partitions).

interviewQuestions.q: Explain the CAP theorem.
interviewQuestions.a: CAP theorem states a distributed system can only guarantee two of three properties: Consistency (all nodes see the same data at the same time), Availability (every request gets a response), and Partition tolerance (system works despite network failures). Since network partitions are inevitable in distributed systems, the real choice is between consistency and availability during partitions.

interviewQuestions.q: Give examples of CP and AP systems.
interviewQuestions.a: CP systems: HBase, MongoDB (in certain configurations), Redis Cluster, Zookeeper — they sacrifice availability during partitions to maintain consistency. AP systems: Cassandra, DynamoDB, CouchDB, DNS — they remain available during partitions but may serve stale data. Most systems allow configuration to tune the CP/AP trade-off.

interviewQuestions.q: Is the CAP theorem still relevant today?
interviewQuestions.a: Yes, but with nuance. Modern systems like Google Spanner achieve near-CA behavior through TrueTime and synchronous replication across datacenters. The PACELC theorem extends CAP: even without partitions, there is a trade-off between Latency and Consistency. In practice, most systems operate on a spectrum rather than strict CP or AP.

tips: Remember: network partitions will happen, so the real choice is always between C and A during a partition.
The PACELC theorem (Partition: A vs C, Else: Latency vs Consistency) is a more complete model — mention it to impress interviewers.

subtopics.title: AP - Availability & Partition Tolerance
subtopics.description: AP systems prioritize availability over consistency. During network partitions, all nodes continue to accept reads and writes, but different nodes may return different values. Eventual consistency ensures data converges once the partition heals.

resources.title: Dynamo: Amazon's Highly Available Key-value Store

interviewQuestions.q: What are AP systems and when should you use them?
interviewQuestions.a: AP systems remain available during partitions at the cost of potentially returning stale data. Use AP when availability is more important than consistency — social media feeds, DNS, shopping carts, content delivery, analytics. Users can tolerate temporarily stale data in these scenarios.

interviewQuestions.q: How do AP systems handle data conflicts after partitions heal?
interviewQuestions.a: They use conflict resolution strategies: last-write-wins (LWW) using timestamps, vector clocks to detect concurrent writes, application-level merge functions (CRDTs), or read-repair where stale replicas are updated when inconsistencies are detected during reads.

interviewQuestions.q: Name some AP databases and their conflict resolution strategies.
interviewQuestions.a: Cassandra uses LWW with timestamps and tunable consistency levels. DynamoDB uses vector clocks and application-level conflict resolution. CouchDB uses revision trees with deterministic winner selection. Riak uses CRDTs (Conflict-free Replicated Data Types) for automatic conflict resolution.

tips: AP is the right default for most web applications — users expect the site to always respond.
When using AP systems, design your data model to minimize conflicts rather than relying solely on conflict resolution.

subtopics.title: CP - Consistency & Partition Tolerance
subtopics.description: CP systems prioritize consistency over availability. During network partitions, the system may refuse requests rather than risk returning stale data. All nodes always return the same, most recent value.

resources.title: Google Spanner - CP at Global Scale

interviewQuestions.q: What are CP systems and when should you use them?
interviewQuestions.a: CP systems guarantee consistency at the cost of availability during partitions. Use CP when incorrect data is unacceptable — banking transactions, inventory systems, booking/reservation systems, distributed locks, configuration management. These scenarios require all nodes to agree on the current state.

interviewQuestions.q: How do CP systems maintain consistency during partitions?
interviewQuestions.a: They use consensus algorithms like Raft or Paxos to ensure all nodes agree before committing writes. During a partition, if a majority quorum cannot be reached, the system rejects writes (and sometimes reads) to prevent inconsistency. The minority partition becomes unavailable until connectivity is restored.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the difference between linearizable and serializable consistency?
interviewQuestions.a: Linearizability means operations appear to execute atomically at some point between invocation and response, respecting real-time ordering. Serializability means transactions appear to execute in some serial order, but not necessarily respecting real-time order. Strict serializability combines both — it is the strongest consistency model.

tips: CP systems typically use quorum-based replication (majority of nodes must acknowledge writes).
In interviews, always justify why you need CP — it comes at the cost of availability and often higher latency.

topics.title: Weak Consistency
topics.description: After a write, reads may or may not see it. A best-effort approach is taken. Weak consistency works well in real-time use cases such as VoIP, video chat, and multiplayer games where losing some data is acceptable.

interviewQuestions.q: What is weak consistency and when is it appropriate?
interviewQuestions.a: Weak consistency means after a write, there is no guarantee that subsequent reads will return the updated value. It is appropriate for real-time applications where speed matters more than accuracy — VoIP calls (dropped packets are acceptable), live video streaming, multiplayer games (minor position glitches are tolerable), and sensor data where approximate readings suffice.

interviewQuestions.q: What are the trade-offs of weak consistency?
interviewQuestions.a: Benefits: lowest latency, highest throughput, simplest implementation, no coordination overhead. Drawbacks: clients may read stale or inconsistent data, no ordering guarantees, application must handle data loss gracefully. It is unsuitable for systems where correctness is critical.

interviewQuestions.q: How does memcached implement weak consistency?
interviewQuestions.a: Memcached provides no replication or consistency guarantees. It is a simple key-value cache — if a node fails, its data is lost. Writes to one client's cache are not visible to other clients until they fetch from the source of truth. This weak consistency model is acceptable because it is a cache, not a primary data store.

tips: Weak consistency is the default for caches and CDNs — and that is perfectly fine for their use cases.
If your system can tolerate data loss (like a game losing a few position updates), weak consistency gives you the best performance.

topics.title: Eventual Consistency
topics.description: After a write, reads will eventually see it (typically within milliseconds). Data is replicated asynchronously. DNS and email are classic examples. Eventual consistency is the most common model in distributed systems.

interviewQuestions.q: What is eventual consistency?
interviewQuestions.a: Eventual consistency guarantees that if no new updates are made to a data item, eventually all replicas will converge to the same value. The window of inconsistency is typically milliseconds to seconds. It is the most practical model for distributed systems, used by DNS, email, Amazon DynamoDB, Apache Cassandra, and most social media platforms.

interviewQuestions.q: How does eventual consistency differ from strong consistency?
interviewQuestions.a: Strong consistency guarantees every read returns the most recent write — all clients see the same data at all times. Eventual consistency allows a temporary window where different clients may see different values. Strong consistency requires synchronous replication (higher latency, lower availability), while eventual consistency uses asynchronous replication (lower latency, higher availability).

interviewQuestions.q: What mechanisms help achieve eventual consistency?
interviewQuestions.a: Anti-entropy protocols (Merkle trees for detecting divergence), read repair (fix stale data when read), hinted handoff (buffer writes for unavailable nodes), gossip protocols (propagate updates between nodes), and vector clocks/version vectors (detect and resolve conflicts).

tips: Eventual consistency is the sweet spot for most web applications — it balances performance with reasonable data freshness.
Design your UI to handle eventual consistency gracefully — show optimistic updates and handle conflict resolution in the background.

topics.title: Strong Consistency
topics.description: After a write, reads will see it. Data is replicated synchronously using consensus protocols. Strong consistency provides the most intuitive behavior but at the cost of higher latency and reduced availability.

resources.title: Google Spanner: Strong Consistency at Scale

interviewQuestions.q: What is strong consistency?
interviewQuestions.a: Strong consistency (linearizability) guarantees that once a write is acknowledged, all subsequent reads from any node return the updated value. It provides the illusion of a single copy of data. Achieved through synchronous replication and consensus protocols like Raft or Paxos.

interviewQuestions.q: What are the costs of strong consistency?
interviewQuestions.a: Higher write latency (must wait for majority/all replicas to acknowledge), reduced availability during partitions (system refuses requests if quorum is not met), lower throughput (synchronous replication is slower), and more complex implementation (consensus protocols are notoriously difficult to implement correctly).

interviewQuestions.q: How does Google Spanner achieve global strong consistency?
interviewQuestions.a: Spanner uses TrueTime, a globally synchronized clock with bounded uncertainty based on GPS and atomic clocks. This allows it to assign globally consistent timestamps to transactions. Combined with synchronous replication across datacenters and two-phase commit, Spanner provides external consistency (stronger than linearizability) at global scale.

tips: Strong consistency is essential for financial systems, distributed locks, and leader election — but overkill for most read-heavy web applications.
If an interviewer asks about consistency, mention the spectrum: weak → eventual → strong → linearizable → strict serializable.

topics.title: Fail-Over
topics.description: Failover is the process of switching to a redundant or standby system upon the failure of the currently active system. It is a critical pattern for achieving high availability in production systems.

interviewQuestions.q: What is failover and why is it important?
interviewQuestions.a: Failover is the automatic or manual switching to a backup system when the primary system fails. It is essential for high availability — without failover, a single component failure can bring down the entire system. Failover minimizes downtime and ensures service continuity.

interviewQuestions.q: What are the main challenges with failover?
interviewQuestions.a: Failover adds complexity: you need health monitoring to detect failures, data synchronization between primary and standby (potential data loss), DNS/routing updates to redirect traffic, and split-brain prevention (both nodes thinking they are primary). There is also a recovery period (failover time) during which service may be degraded.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you detect when failover is needed?
interviewQuestions.a: Health checks (HTTP pings, TCP checks), heartbeat monitoring between nodes, application-level health indicators, resource monitoring (CPU, memory, disk), and external monitoring services. Use multiple health check methods to avoid false positives — a node might fail health checks due to network issues, not actual failure.

tips: Always test your failover process regularly — an untested failover is worse than no failover because you have false confidence.
Design for graceful degradation — partial service is better than total outage.

subtopics.title: Active-Active
subtopics.description: In active-active failover, both servers handle traffic simultaneously. If one fails, the other absorbs all traffic. This provides better resource utilization and lower latency but requires more complex data synchronization.

interviewQuestions.q: What is active-active failover?
interviewQuestions.a: Both servers actively handle traffic simultaneously, typically behind a load balancer. If one server fails, the other handles all traffic with no failover delay. Benefits: better resource utilization, lower latency (load is distributed), instant failover. Challenges: data synchronization, conflict resolution for concurrent writes, more complex configuration.

interviewQuestions.q: When should you use active-active over active-passive?
interviewQuestions.a: Use active-active when you need maximum resource utilization, cannot tolerate any failover downtime, have read-heavy workloads that can be distributed, or need multi-region deployment for latency reduction. Active-passive is simpler and preferred when you have write-heavy workloads or strict consistency requirements.

interviewQuestions.q: How does Netflix achieve active-active across regions?
interviewQuestions.a: Netflix uses active-active across multiple AWS regions. Each region serves its local users independently. Data is replicated asynchronously using Cassandra (AP system). If one region fails, DNS is updated to route traffic to healthy regions. They use stateless services, regional data partitioning, and chaos engineering (Chaos Monkey) to ensure resilience.

tips: Active-active is ideal for read-heavy systems — writes are the hard part due to conflict resolution.
For multi-region active-active, use DNS-based routing to direct users to their nearest region.

subtopics.title: Active-Passive
subtopics.description: In active-passive failover, the active server handles all traffic while the passive (standby) server waits. If the active server fails, the passive takes over. Simpler but less resource-efficient.

youtubeVideos.title: Hot vs Warm vs Cold Standby

interviewQuestions.q: What is active-passive failover?
interviewQuestions.a: One server (active/primary) handles all traffic while another (passive/standby) stays idle, receiving replicated data. If the primary fails, the standby is promoted. Types: Hot standby (real-time replication, instant failover), Warm standby (periodic sync, minutes to failover), Cold standby (no sync, hours to restore from backup).

interviewQuestions.q: What are the disadvantages of active-passive?
interviewQuestions.a: The passive server is idle, wasting resources. There is a failover window during which service is degraded or unavailable. Data replication lag may cause data loss. DNS TTL can delay traffic redirection. The failover process itself can fail if not regularly tested.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you minimize data loss during active-passive failover?
interviewQuestions.a: Use synchronous replication (zero data loss but higher latency), minimize replication lag with streaming replication, implement write-ahead logs (WAL) for crash recovery, use shared storage (SAN) so both nodes access the same data, or use semi-synchronous replication as a compromise between sync and async.

tips: Active-passive is simpler and cheaper — use it unless you need the benefits of active-active.
Always configure automated health checks and failover — manual failover during an outage is error-prone.

topics.title: Replication
topics.description: Replication involves maintaining copies of data across multiple servers. It improves availability, read performance, and fault tolerance. The two main patterns are master-slave and master-master replication.

interviewQuestions.q: What is database replication?
interviewQuestions.a: Database replication is maintaining copies of data on multiple servers. Reads can be distributed across replicas for better performance. If one replica fails, others can serve requests. Types include synchronous (write acknowledged after all replicas update) and asynchronous (write acknowledged immediately, replicas updated later).

interviewQuestions.q: What are the trade-offs between synchronous and asynchronous replication?
interviewQuestions.a: Synchronous: zero data loss, strong consistency, but higher write latency and reduced availability if a replica is slow/down. Asynchronous: lower write latency, higher availability, but potential data loss if primary fails before replicas catch up, and temporary inconsistency between replicas.

interviewQuestions.q: How does replication differ from sharding?
interviewQuestions.a: Replication copies the same data across multiple servers for availability and read performance. Sharding splits data across servers so each holds a portion for write scalability. They are complementary — you often use both: shard for write scaling, then replicate each shard for read scaling and availability.

tips: Replication improves read performance and availability; sharding improves write performance and storage capacity. Use both for large-scale systems.
Monitor replication lag — it is a key indicator of system health in replicated databases.

subtopics.title: Master-Slave Replication
subtopics.description: The master serves reads and writes. Slaves replicate from master and serve reads only. If the master goes offline, a slave can be promoted. Simple and widely used.

interviewQuestions.q: How does master-slave replication work?
interviewQuestions.a: The master handles all write operations and replicates changes to one or more slaves. Slaves are read-only and serve read queries, distributing read load. If the master fails, a slave can be promoted to master (manual or automatic failover). Common in MySQL, PostgreSQL, MongoDB, and Redis.

interviewQuestions.q: What are the limitations of master-slave replication?
interviewQuestions.a: Single point of failure for writes (master bottleneck), replication lag means slaves may serve stale data, failover requires promoting a slave and reconfiguring other slaves, and additional write logic if you need to handle master failure without data loss.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you handle master failure in master-slave replication?
interviewQuestions.a: Promote a slave to master (choose the one with least replication lag), reconfigure remaining slaves to replicate from the new master, update application connection strings or use a virtual IP/proxy. Automated tools like MySQL MHA, PostgreSQL Patroni, or cloud-managed failover (AWS RDS Multi-AZ) handle this automatically.

tips: Use read replicas to offload read-heavy queries like analytics and reporting.
Place read replicas in different availability zones or regions for geographic distribution.

subtopics.title: Master-Master Replication
subtopics.description: Both masters serve reads and writes, coordinating with each other. Provides better write availability but introduces conflict resolution complexity.

interviewQuestions.q: How does master-master replication work?
interviewQuestions.a: Both (or all) nodes accept writes and replicate to each other. Provides higher write availability — if one master fails, the other continues serving writes. Requires conflict resolution for concurrent writes to the same data. Used in multi-region deployments where each region needs local write capability.

interviewQuestions.q: What are the challenges of master-master replication?
interviewQuestions.a: Write conflicts when both masters update the same record simultaneously, increased complexity for conflict resolution, potential for data divergence, higher replication traffic, and more complex monitoring. Auto-increment IDs require special handling (odd/even, UUIDs, or distributed ID generation).

interviewQuestions.q: How do you resolve write conflicts in master-master replication?
interviewQuestions.a: Strategies include: Last-Write-Wins (LWW) using timestamps (simple but can lose data), custom merge functions (application-specific logic), CRDTs (Conflict-free Replicated Data Types for automatic merging), and avoiding conflicts by partitioning writes (each master owns specific records/regions).

tips: Master-master is powerful for multi-region deployments but adds significant complexity — only use it when you truly need write availability in multiple locations.
To avoid conflicts, partition writes by region or entity type so each master owns specific data.

topics.title: Availability in Numbers
topics.description: Availability is typically measured as a percentage of uptime. The 'nines' of availability define how much downtime is acceptable — from 99% (3.65 days/year) to 99.999% (5.26 minutes/year).

resources.title: Availability in Numbers - System Design Primer

interviewQuestions.q: What do the nines of availability mean?
interviewQuestions.a: 99% = 3.65 days downtime/year, 99.9% = 8.77 hours/year, 99.99% = 52.6 minutes/year, 99.999% = 5.26 minutes/year, 99.9999% = 31.6 seconds/year. Each additional nine requires exponentially more investment in redundancy, monitoring, and engineering effort.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you calculate the availability of a system with multiple components?
interviewQuestions.a: For components in sequence (all must work): multiply availabilities. Three 99.9% components in series = 0.999^3 = 99.7%. For components in parallel (any can serve): 1 - (1-a1)(1-a2). Two 99.9% components in parallel = 1 - 0.001^2 = 99.9999%. This is why redundancy dramatically improves availability.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the difference between SLA, SLO, and SLI?
interviewQuestions.a: SLI (Service Level Indicator) is the actual measured metric (e.g., 99.95% requests served under 200ms). SLO (Service Level Objective) is the target for that metric (e.g., 99.9% of requests under 200ms). SLA (Service Level Agreement) is the contract with customers specifying consequences if SLOs are not met (e.g., refunds/credits).

tips: Memorize the nines table — it is frequently referenced in interviews and helps you set realistic availability targets.
Remember: components in series multiply (reduce) availability, components in parallel increase it — this is why redundancy matters.

topics.title: Event-Driven Architecture
topics.description: Event-driven systems react to events (state changes) rather than polling. Components communicate through events, enabling loose coupling and better scalability. Events are immutable facts about something that happened.

interviewQuestions.q: What is event-driven architecture?
interviewQuestions.a: Event-driven architecture is a design pattern where the flow of the program is determined by events — significant changes in state. Producers emit events without knowing who will consume them. Consumers subscribe to events they care about. This decouples producers from consumers, enabling independent scaling and deployment. Common implementations use message brokers like Kafka, RabbitMQ, or AWS EventBridge.

interviewQuestions.q: What are the benefits of event-driven architecture?
interviewQuestions.a: Loose coupling between services, better scalability (consumers scale independently), natural audit trail (events are immutable facts), flexibility to add new consumers without modifying producers, and ability to replay events for debugging or rebuilding state. It also enables CQRS and Event Sourcing patterns.

interviewQuestions.q: What are the challenges of event-driven architecture?
interviewQuestions.a: Eventual consistency (events take time to propagate), debugging is harder (distributed tracing needed), event ordering can be complex, duplicate event handling (idempotency required), schema evolution of events over time, and increased infrastructure complexity (message brokers, dead letter queues).

tips: Design events as immutable facts about what happened, not as commands about what to do.
Always handle duplicate events gracefully — implement idempotent consumers.

topics.title: Schedule-Driven Jobs
topics.description: Schedule-driven jobs run at predetermined times or intervals using cron-like scheduling. Common for batch processing, report generation, data cleanup, and periodic health checks.

interviewQuestions.q: What are schedule-driven jobs and when should you use them?
interviewQuestions.a: Schedule-driven jobs execute at fixed times or intervals (cron expressions). Use them for batch processing (nightly data aggregation), periodic cleanup (delete expired sessions), report generation (daily sales reports), health checks, cache warming, and data synchronization between systems.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you handle scheduled jobs in a distributed system?
interviewQuestions.a: Use distributed job schedulers (Quartz Cluster, Hangfire, Temporal, AWS EventBridge Scheduler) that ensure only one instance runs the job. Implement distributed locking (Redis, Zookeeper) to prevent duplicate execution. Use leader election so only one node schedules jobs. Store job state in a shared database for visibility.

interviewQuestions.q: What happens if a scheduled job fails or takes longer than expected?
interviewQuestions.a: Implement retry with backoff, dead letter queues for failed jobs, alerting on failures, idempotent job execution (safe to re-run), timeout mechanisms, and overlap prevention (skip if previous run is still active). Log job duration and success/failure metrics for monitoring.

tips: Always make scheduled jobs idempotent — they should produce the same result if run multiple times.
Use distributed locking to prevent the same job from running on multiple instances in a scaled-out environment.

topics.title: Returning Results from Background Jobs
topics.description: Background jobs need mechanisms to communicate results back to the caller or downstream systems. Patterns include callbacks, polling, webhooks, and event-driven notifications.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you return results from background jobs?
interviewQuestions.a: Common patterns: 1) Polling — client checks a status endpoint periodically, 2) Webhooks — job calls back a URL when done, 3) WebSocket/SSE — push notification to connected client, 4) Event/Message — publish completion event to a queue, 5) Status table — store results in DB, client queries when needed.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the async request-reply pattern?
interviewQuestions.a: The client submits a request and receives an immediate 202 Accepted response with a status URL. The client polls the status URL periodically. When the job completes, the status URL returns the result or a link to it. This decouples the client from the processing time of long-running operations.

interviewQuestions.q: When should you use webhooks vs polling for job results?
interviewQuestions.a: Use webhooks when: clients can receive HTTP callbacks, you want real-time notification, and you can handle delivery failures. Use polling when: clients cannot expose endpoints (browsers, mobile apps), simplicity is preferred, or the result is needed on-demand. Webhooks are more efficient but require retry logic and idempotency.

tips: For web UIs, combine polling with optimistic updates — show a spinner and poll every few seconds.
Always include a timeout mechanism — background jobs should not run indefinitely.

topics.title: Domain Name System (DNS)
topics.description: DNS translates domain names to IP addresses. It is a hierarchical, distributed system that is fundamental to the internet. Understanding DNS is critical for system design — it affects latency, availability, and load distribution.

youtubeVideos.title: DNS Deep Dive for System Design

interviewQuestions.q: Explain how DNS resolution works.
interviewQuestions.a: 1) Browser checks its cache, then OS cache, then router cache. 2) If not found, queries the configured DNS resolver (usually ISP's). 3) Resolver queries root nameserver → TLD nameserver (.com) → authoritative nameserver for the domain. 4) Authoritative server returns the IP address. 5) Each step caches the result according to the TTL. Total resolution typically takes 20-120ms for uncached queries.

interviewQuestions.q: What are DNS record types and when do you use each?
interviewQuestions.a: A record: maps domain to IPv4 address. AAAA: maps to IPv6. CNAME: maps domain to another domain (alias). MX: mail server routing. NS: delegates a subdomain to nameservers. TXT: verification and SPF/DKIM records. SRV: service discovery. PTR: reverse DNS lookup. In system design, A records and CNAME records are most commonly used for load balancing and service routing.

interviewQuestions.q: How is DNS used for load balancing?
interviewQuestions.a: DNS-based load balancing returns different IP addresses for the same domain: Round-robin DNS rotates through a list of IPs, weighted DNS returns IPs based on configured weights, latency-based DNS returns the IP closest to the user, and failover DNS returns a backup IP when the primary is unhealthy. Services like Route 53, Cloudflare DNS, and Azure Traffic Manager implement these strategies.

tips: DNS is eventually consistent — TTL determines how long stale entries persist. Keep TTL low (60-300s) for services that need quick failover.
Use managed DNS services (Route 53, Cloudflare) for production — they provide global anycast, health checks, and weighted routing.

topics.title: Content Delivery Networks (CDN)
topics.description: A CDN is a globally distributed network of proxy servers that serve content from locations closer to users. CDNs improve performance by reducing latency, offloading traffic from origin servers, and providing DDoS protection.

interviewQuestions.q: What is a CDN and why is it important?
interviewQuestions.a: A CDN is a network of edge servers distributed globally that cache and serve content close to users. Benefits: reduced latency (content served from nearest edge), reduced origin load (edge servers handle most requests), improved availability (redundant edge locations), DDoS protection (distributed absorption), and better user experience worldwide.

interviewQuestions.q: When should you use a CDN?
interviewQuestions.a: Use CDNs for: static assets (images, CSS, JS, videos), frequently accessed API responses, geographically distributed users, high-traffic websites, and any content that can be cached. Consider skipping CDN for: highly dynamic/personalized content, very small user base in one region, or real-time data that cannot tolerate any caching delay.

interviewQuestions.q: How do CDNs handle cache invalidation?
interviewQuestions.a: Methods include: TTL-based expiration (content refreshed after time period), cache purge API (manually invalidate specific URLs or patterns), versioned URLs (change filename/query string for new versions), cache-control headers (max-age, no-cache, stale-while-revalidate), and tag-based purging (invalidate by content category). Versioned URLs are the most reliable approach.

tips: Use versioned filenames (style.abc123.css) instead of cache busting with query strings — some CDNs and proxies ignore query strings.
Set appropriate cache-control headers: long max-age for versioned assets, short for API responses, no-cache for personalized content.

subtopics.title: Push CDNs
subtopics.description: With push CDNs, you upload content directly to the CDN when it changes. You manage when content is updated and expired. Best for sites with content that does not change frequently.

interviewQuestions.q: How do push CDNs work?
interviewQuestions.a: With push CDNs, you proactively upload content to the CDN nodes. When content changes, you push the updated version. The CDN serves whatever was last pushed. You control exactly what content is on the CDN and when it changes. Minimizes storage (only what you push is stored) and provides predictable cache behavior.

interviewQuestions.q: When should you use push CDNs over pull CDNs?
interviewQuestions.a: Use push CDNs when: content changes infrequently (documentation, firmware, large media files), you need precise control over cached content, traffic is low to moderate (pull CDN's automatic caching has less benefit), and storage costs matter (only pushed content takes up space).

interviewQuestions.q: What are the disadvantages of push CDNs?
interviewQuestions.a: You must manage the push process (CI/CD integration), content updates require explicit action, higher operational overhead, and if you forget to push updated content, users see stale versions. Not ideal for high-traffic, frequently changing content where automatic caching is more efficient.

tips: Push CDNs work great with CI/CD pipelines — push new assets as part of your deployment process.
Use push CDNs for large files that rarely change (videos, installers) to avoid expensive origin fetches.

subtopics.title: Pull CDNs
subtopics.description: Pull CDNs fetch content from the origin server on the first request and cache it. Subsequent requests are served from the CDN cache until the TTL expires. Easier to manage for high-traffic, frequently changing content.

interviewQuestions.q: How do pull CDNs work?
interviewQuestions.a: When a user requests content, the CDN edge checks its cache. On a cache miss, it fetches from the origin server, caches the response, and serves it. Subsequent requests hit the cache. When the TTL expires, the next request triggers a fresh fetch. The CDN automatically manages what is cached based on request patterns.

interviewQuestions.q: When should you use pull CDNs?
interviewQuestions.a: Use pull CDNs when: traffic is high (automatic caching is very efficient), content changes frequently (TTL manages freshness), you want minimal operational overhead (no need to push content), and for websites with unpredictable content access patterns. Pull CDNs are the default choice for most web applications.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the cold cache problem with pull CDNs?
interviewQuestions.a: When CDN cache is empty (new edge location, TTL expired, cache evicted), the first request goes to origin — called a cache miss. This causes higher latency for that user. Mitigation: cache warming (pre-fetch popular content), origin shield (centralized cache between edge and origin), and stale-while-revalidate (serve stale content while fetching fresh version in background).

tips: Pull CDNs are the standard choice for most web applications — set up Cloudflare or CloudFront and let them handle caching automatically.
Use the stale-while-revalidate cache-control directive to eliminate cache miss latency for popular content.

topics.title: Load Balancers
topics.description: Load balancers distribute incoming network traffic across multiple servers to ensure no single server bears too much load. They improve availability and reliability by distributing the workload evenly.

interviewQuestions.q: What is a load balancer and why is it needed?
interviewQuestions.a: A load balancer distributes incoming requests across multiple servers. Benefits: prevents any single server from being overwhelmed, improves response time by routing to less loaded servers, provides high availability (routes around failed servers), enables horizontal scaling (add/remove servers transparently), and can terminate SSL for backend servers.

interviewQuestions.q: What happens if the load balancer itself fails?
interviewQuestions.a: Load balancers are a potential single point of failure. To mitigate: deploy load balancers in pairs (active-passive or active-active), use DNS-based failover between LB instances, employ cloud-managed LBs (AWS ALB/NLB, Azure LB) which are inherently highly available, and use health checks to detect and route around LB failures.

interviewQuestions.q: How does a load balancer decide which server to route to?
interviewQuestions.a: Common algorithms: Round Robin (rotate sequentially), Weighted Round Robin (proportional to server capacity), Least Connections (route to server with fewest active connections), IP Hash (same client always goes to same server — session persistence), Least Response Time (route to fastest server), and Random.

tips: In system design interviews, always mention load balancers when discussing horizontal scaling — they are the entry point for distributed traffic.
Use health checks to automatically remove unhealthy servers from the pool and add them back when they recover.

subtopics.title: Load Balancer vs Reverse Proxy
subtopics.description: While both sit between clients and servers, a load balancer distributes traffic across multiple servers, while a reverse proxy can provide additional features like caching, SSL termination, and compression even with a single server.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the difference between a load balancer and a reverse proxy?
interviewQuestions.a: A load balancer distributes requests across multiple servers — it requires multiple backend servers. A reverse proxy sits in front of servers and provides features like caching, SSL termination, compression, and request routing — useful even with a single backend server. In practice, most modern tools (NGINX, HAProxy, cloud LBs) serve both roles.

interviewQuestions.q: What is a reverse proxy and what benefits does it provide?
interviewQuestions.a: A reverse proxy is a server that sits between clients and backend servers. Benefits: SSL termination (offload encryption), caching (reduce backend load), compression (reduce bandwidth), request routing (URL-based routing to different backends), security (hide backend topology, rate limiting, WAF), and logging/monitoring.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the difference between a forward proxy and a reverse proxy?
interviewQuestions.a: A forward proxy acts on behalf of clients — it sits between users and the internet (e.g., corporate proxy, VPN). A reverse proxy acts on behalf of servers — it sits between the internet and your servers. Clients know they are using a forward proxy; clients typically do not know a reverse proxy exists.

tips: Even with a single server, a reverse proxy adds value through SSL termination, caching, and security features.
NGINX is the most popular choice for both reverse proxy and load balancing — learn its configuration.

subtopics.title: Load Balancing Algorithms
subtopics.description: Different algorithms distribute traffic in different ways. The best algorithm depends on your workload characteristics — whether servers are homogeneous, whether sessions need persistence, and whether you need to minimize latency.

interviewQuestions.q: Explain the common load balancing algorithms.
interviewQuestions.a: Round Robin: sequential rotation (simplest). Weighted Round Robin: proportional to server capacity. Least Connections: route to server with fewest active connections (good for long-lived connections). Least Response Time: route to fastest responding server. IP Hash: hash client IP for consistent routing (session persistence). Consistent Hashing: minimizes redistribution when servers are added/removed.

interviewQuestions.q: When would you use least connections vs round robin?
interviewQuestions.a: Use Round Robin for stateless, homogeneous servers with similar request durations. Use Least Connections when requests have varying processing times (some requests are quick, others take seconds) — it prevents slow requests from overloading a server while others are idle.

interviewQuestions.q: What is consistent hashing and why is it important for load balancing?
interviewQuestions.a: Consistent hashing maps both servers and requests to a hash ring. Each request is routed to the next server clockwise on the ring. When a server is added/removed, only 1/N keys need to be redistributed (vs. all keys with modular hashing). This is crucial for caching layers and stateful services where redistributing keys would cause cache storms.

tips: Least Connections is usually the best default for web applications with varying response times.
Use Consistent Hashing when routing to caches — it minimizes cache misses when the server pool changes.

subtopics.title: Layer 7 Load Balancing
subtopics.description: Layer 7 (application layer) load balancers make routing decisions based on HTTP headers, URL paths, cookies, and request content. They can perform content-based routing, SSL termination, and header manipulation.

interviewQuestions.q: What is Layer 7 load balancing?
interviewQuestions.a: Layer 7 operates at the application layer (HTTP/HTTPS). It can inspect request content — URL path, headers, cookies, query parameters, and body. This enables content-based routing (route /api to API servers, /static to CDN), A/B testing (route by cookie), canary deployments (route 5% to new version), and authentication offloading.

interviewQuestions.q: What are the advantages of Layer 7 over Layer 4?
interviewQuestions.a: Layer 7 provides: content-based routing (URL path, headers), SSL termination at the LB, HTTP caching, request/response manipulation (add headers, rewrite URLs), WebSocket support with HTTP upgrade, health checks at application level, and better logging/monitoring of application traffic.

interviewQuestions.q: When should you choose Layer 7 over Layer 4 load balancing?
interviewQuestions.a: Choose Layer 7 when: you need URL-based routing (microservices), require SSL termination at the LB, want HTTP-level features (caching, compression, header manipulation), need application-level health checks, or are implementing A/B testing/canary deployments. Choose Layer 4 for maximum performance, non-HTTP protocols, or when request inspection is not needed.

tips: Layer 7 LBs are the standard for modern web applications — use them unless you have specific needs for Layer 4 performance.
Use path-based routing to direct traffic to different microservices through a single domain and load balancer.

subtopics.title: Layer 4 Load Balancing
subtopics.description: Layer 4 (transport layer) load balancers make routing decisions based on IP addresses and TCP/UDP ports without inspecting packet content. They are faster and more efficient but less flexible than Layer 7.

interviewQuestions.q: What is Layer 4 load balancing?
interviewQuestions.a: Layer 4 operates at the transport layer (TCP/UDP). It routes traffic based on source/destination IP and port without inspecting packet content. It simply forwards TCP/UDP connections to backend servers. Advantages: very high performance (no content inspection overhead), works with any protocol (not just HTTP), lower latency, and simpler configuration.

interviewQuestions.q: What are the limitations of Layer 4 load balancing?
interviewQuestions.a: Cannot route based on content (URL, headers, cookies), cannot terminate SSL (encrypted traffic is opaque), cannot cache or compress content, limited health check capability (TCP port check only, not application-level), and cannot modify requests/responses. It treats all traffic on the same port identically.

interviewQuestions.q: Give a scenario where Layer 4 is preferred over Layer 7.
interviewQuestions.a: Layer 4 is preferred for: gaming servers (UDP traffic), database connection pooling (MySQL, PostgreSQL), any non-HTTP protocol (MQTT, custom TCP), scenarios requiring maximum throughput with minimal latency, passing through encrypted traffic without termination, and when you need to preserve the original client IP (DSR/Direct Server Return).

tips: Use Layer 4 LBs (AWS NLB) for non-HTTP services, database proxying, and ultra-low-latency requirements.
Layer 4 LBs handle millions of connections per second — use them as the first tier in a multi-tier LB architecture.

topics.title: Application Layer
topics.description: Scaling the application layer involves running multiple instances of your application behind a load balancer. Stateless design is key — all state should be externalized to databases, caches, or session stores.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you scale the application layer horizontally?
interviewQuestions.a: Make the application stateless — move all state (sessions, file uploads, caches) to external stores (Redis, S3, databases). Deploy multiple instances behind a load balancer. Use auto-scaling based on CPU/memory/request metrics. Implement health checks so the LB removes unhealthy instances. Use container orchestration (Kubernetes) for automated scaling and management.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the Twelve-Factor App methodology?
interviewQuestions.a: A set of 12 best practices for building scalable applications: 1) One codebase per app, 2) Explicitly declare dependencies, 3) Store config in environment, 4) Treat backing services as attached resources, 5) Strict separation of build/release/run, 6) Execute as stateless processes, 7) Export services via port binding, 8) Scale via process model, 9) Fast startup/graceful shutdown, 10) Dev/prod parity, 11) Treat logs as event streams, 12) Run admin tasks as one-off processes.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you handle file uploads in a horizontally scaled application?
interviewQuestions.a: Never store files on the local filesystem — any instance might handle the request. Use shared storage: object storage (AWS S3, Azure Blob), NFS/shared filesystem, or a CDN with direct upload. For large files, use multipart upload with signed URLs so clients upload directly to storage, bypassing the application server entirely.

tips: The #1 rule of horizontal scaling: make everything stateless. Push all state to databases, caches, and object storage.
Use readiness and liveness probes in Kubernetes to ensure traffic only goes to healthy, ready instances.

topics.title: Microservices
topics.description: Microservices architecture decomposes an application into small, independent services that communicate over the network. Each service owns its data and can be deployed, scaled, and maintained independently.

interviewQuestions.q: What are microservices and when should you use them?
interviewQuestions.a: Microservices decompose a monolith into small, independently deployable services, each owning its data and business logic. Use them when: the team is large enough to own separate services, different parts of the system need different scaling, you want independent deployment cycles, or different services benefit from different technology stacks. Avoid for small teams or simple applications — the operational overhead is significant.

interviewQuestions.q: What are the challenges of microservices architecture?
interviewQuestions.a: Distributed system complexity (network failures, latency), data consistency across services (no shared database), operational overhead (monitoring, logging, tracing for N services), service discovery and communication, API versioning, distributed transactions (saga pattern), testing complexity (integration tests across services), and the need for DevOps maturity.

interviewQuestions.q: How do microservices communicate with each other?
interviewQuestions.a: Synchronous: REST APIs (HTTP/JSON), gRPC (binary, efficient), GraphQL. Asynchronous: message queues (RabbitMQ, SQS), event streaming (Kafka, AWS EventBridge). Choose synchronous for request-reply patterns and asynchronous for fire-and-forget, event-driven, or when services need to be decoupled. Most systems use a mix of both.

tips: Start with a modular monolith and extract microservices when you have a clear need — premature decomposition is expensive.
Each microservice should be small enough that one team can own it, but large enough to provide meaningful business value.

topics.title: Service Discovery
topics.description: Service discovery is how services find each other in a dynamic environment where instances come and go. It is essential in microservices and container orchestration platforms.

interviewQuestions.q: What is service discovery and why is it needed?
interviewQuestions.a: Service discovery is the process of automatically detecting service instances and their network locations. In dynamic environments (containers, auto-scaling), IP addresses and ports change frequently. Service discovery replaces hardcoded addresses with a registry that tracks available instances. Without it, services cannot communicate reliably in dynamic infrastructure.

interviewQuestions.q: What are the two main patterns for service discovery?
interviewQuestions.a: Client-side discovery: the client queries a service registry (Consul, Eureka) to find available instances and load-balances directly. Server-side discovery: the client sends requests to a load balancer/router that queries the registry and routes the request. Kubernetes uses server-side discovery via kube-proxy and DNS.

interviewQuestions.q: What is a service mesh and how does it relate to service discovery?
interviewQuestions.a: A service mesh (Istio, Linkerd, Consul Connect) is an infrastructure layer that handles service-to-service communication. It provides: automatic service discovery, load balancing, TLS encryption, circuit breaking, retries, observability (metrics, tracing, logging), and traffic management (canary deployments, A/B testing). It runs as sidecar proxies alongside each service, requiring no application code changes.

tips: In Kubernetes, service discovery is built-in via DNS — each Service gets a DNS name that resolves to healthy pod IPs.
A service mesh (Istio, Linkerd) adds powerful networking features but also adds complexity — only adopt it when you need its features.

topics.title: SQL vs NoSQL
topics.description: SQL databases are relational, use structured schemas and SQL for queries. NoSQL databases are non-relational, offering flexible schemas and various data models. Choosing between them depends on data structure, scale, and consistency requirements.

youtubeVideos.title: When to Use SQL vs NoSQL

resources.title: SQL vs NoSQL - System Design Primer

interviewQuestions.q: When should you use SQL vs NoSQL?
interviewQuestions.a: Use SQL when: data is structured with clear relationships, you need ACID transactions, complex queries with JOINs are common, data integrity is critical (financial systems). Use NoSQL when: schema evolves rapidly, data is semi-structured or unstructured, you need horizontal scalability, high write throughput is required, or data access patterns are simple key-value or document lookups.

interviewQuestions.q: Can you use both SQL and NoSQL in the same system?
interviewQuestions.a: Yes, polyglot persistence is common. Use SQL for transactional data (orders, payments), NoSQL document store for product catalogs, Redis for caching/sessions, Elasticsearch for search, and a graph database for recommendations. Each database type excels at different access patterns.

interviewQuestions.q: What are the ACID properties and why do they matter?
interviewQuestions.a: Atomicity: transactions are all-or-nothing. Consistency: data moves from one valid state to another. Isolation: concurrent transactions don't interfere. Durability: committed data survives failures. ACID guarantees are crucial for financial systems, booking systems, and any application where partial updates would cause data corruption.

tips: Default to SQL unless you have a specific reason for NoSQL — relational databases are incredibly versatile and well-understood.
In interviews, justify your database choice based on access patterns, not just scale.

topics.title: Database Replication
topics.description: Database replication maintains copies of data across multiple servers for high availability, read scaling, and disaster recovery.

interviewQuestions.q: What are the types of database replication?
interviewQuestions.a: Synchronous: all replicas must confirm write before it is acknowledged (strong consistency, higher latency). Asynchronous: write is acknowledged immediately, replicas updated later (eventual consistency, lower latency). Semi-synchronous: at least one replica confirms (compromise). Statement-based: replays SQL statements. Row-based: replicates actual data changes. Logical: application-level replication.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you handle replication lag?
interviewQuestions.a: Monitor lag metrics, use read-your-own-writes consistency (route reads to primary after writes), use monotonic reads (ensure client reads from same replica), implement causal consistency where needed, set up alerts for excessive lag, and scale read replicas to reduce individual load.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the split-brain problem in replication?
interviewQuestions.a: Split-brain occurs when network partition causes both nodes to think they are the primary, accepting writes independently. This leads to data divergence. Prevention: use quorum-based systems (majority must agree), fencing mechanisms (STONITH), shared storage with locks, or external arbitrators (witness nodes).

tips: Always monitor replication lag — it is the #1 indicator of replication health.
Use read replicas for analytics and reporting queries to offload the primary.

topics.title: Database Sharding
topics.description: Sharding distributes data across multiple databases so each holds a subset. This enables horizontal write scaling beyond what a single server can handle.

interviewQuestions.q: What is database sharding and when do you need it?
interviewQuestions.a: Sharding splits data across multiple databases using a shard key. Each shard holds a subset of data. Need it when: a single database cannot handle write volume, data size exceeds single server storage, you need geographic data locality. Common shard keys: user ID, geographic region, tenant ID. Choose keys that distribute data evenly and align with query patterns.

interviewQuestions.q: What are the challenges of sharding?
interviewQuestions.a: Cross-shard queries are expensive (JOINs across shards), rebalancing data when adding shards is complex, maintaining referential integrity across shards, uneven data distribution (hotspots), increased operational complexity, and application must be shard-aware. Some operations become impossible or very expensive (global sorting, aggregation).

interviewQuestions.q: What sharding strategies exist?
interviewQuestions.a: Range-based: shard by key ranges (users A-M, N-Z) — easy to implement but can cause hotspots. Hash-based: hash the shard key (consistent hashing) — even distribution but range queries are hard. Directory-based: lookup table maps keys to shards — flexible but the directory is a bottleneck/SPOF. Geographic: shard by region — good for data locality requirements.

tips: Avoid sharding as long as possible — try vertical scaling, read replicas, caching, and query optimization first.
Choose a shard key that matches your most common query pattern and distributes data evenly.

topics.title: Database Federation
topics.description: Federation (functional partitioning) splits databases by function. For example, separate databases for users, products, and orders. Each database handles its own read and write traffic.

interviewQuestions.q: What is database federation?
interviewQuestions.a: Federation splits databases by function — a users database, products database, orders database, etc. Each functional database handles its own traffic independently. Benefits: smaller databases are faster, each can be scaled independently, failure isolation, and each team can own their database. Drawbacks: cross-function JOINs require application-level joins, and more complex infrastructure.

interviewQuestions.q: How does federation differ from sharding?
interviewQuestions.a: Federation splits by function (users DB, orders DB) — each database has different tables. Sharding splits by data (users 1-1M on shard 1, users 1M-2M on shard 2) — each shard has the same tables. Federation is simpler to implement but has limited scalability per function. Sharding is more complex but can scale each function further.

interviewQuestions.q: When should you use federation?
interviewQuestions.a: When different functional areas have different scaling needs, different teams own different domains (microservices), you want failure isolation between domains, and cross-domain queries are rare. It aligns naturally with Domain-Driven Design and microservices architecture where each bounded context owns its data.

tips: Federation aligns naturally with microservices — each service owns its database.
Combine federation with sharding: federate by function first, then shard individual databases that grow too large.

topics.title: Denormalization
topics.description: Denormalization adds redundant data to one or more tables to avoid expensive JOINs. It trades write complexity and storage for read performance.

interviewQuestions.q: What is denormalization and when should you use it?
interviewQuestions.a: Denormalization stores redundant copies of data to avoid JOINs at read time. Use it when: read performance is critical and JOINs are a bottleneck, your system is read-heavy (10:1 or higher read-to-write ratio), you are using a NoSQL database that does not support JOINs, or after federation/sharding makes cross-database JOINs impossible.

interviewQuestions.q: What are the risks of denormalization?
interviewQuestions.a: Data redundancy increases storage costs, write operations become more complex (must update all copies), risk of data inconsistency if updates miss a copy, harder to maintain data integrity, and schema changes require updating multiple locations. Mitigate with: materialized views, change data capture (CDC), or application-level consistency checks.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you keep denormalized data consistent?
interviewQuestions.a: Approaches: application-level updates (update all copies in a transaction), change data capture (CDC) to propagate changes asynchronously, materialized views that auto-refresh, event-driven updates via message queues, and periodic reconciliation jobs that detect and fix inconsistencies.

tips: Normalize first, then denormalize specific read paths that are proven bottlenecks — do not denormalize prematurely.
Use materialized views or CDC (Change Data Capture) to keep denormalized data in sync automatically.

topics.title: SQL Tuning
topics.description: SQL tuning involves optimizing queries, indexes, and database configuration for better performance. It is often the first step before considering more complex scaling strategies.

interviewQuestions.q: What are the key SQL tuning strategies?
interviewQuestions.a: 1) Add proper indexes (B-tree for equality/range, hash for equality, composite for multi-column), 2) Use EXPLAIN/ANALYZE to understand query plans, 3) Avoid SELECT * — fetch only needed columns, 4) Optimize JOINs (use indexed columns, prefer smaller result sets), 5) Use connection pooling, 6) Tune database configuration (buffer pool, query cache), 7) Partition large tables, 8) Use read replicas for analytics.

interviewQuestions.q: How do database indexes work?
interviewQuestions.a: Indexes are data structures (typically B-trees) that allow the database to find rows without scanning the entire table. A B-tree index maintains sorted data enabling O(log n) lookups. Composite indexes support queries on the leftmost prefix of columns. Indexes speed up reads but slow down writes (index must be updated). Too many indexes waste storage and slow INSERT/UPDATE operations.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the N+1 query problem?
interviewQuestions.a: N+1 occurs when code fetches a list of N items, then makes one additional query per item to fetch related data — resulting in N+1 total queries instead of 1-2. Fix with: eager loading (JOIN in one query), batch loading (WHERE id IN (...)), or GraphQL/DataLoader pattern. This is one of the most common performance issues in web applications.

tips: Always check EXPLAIN ANALYZE output before and after adding indexes — verify the optimizer actually uses them.
The N+1 query problem is the most common performance issue — always batch or eager-load related data.

topics.title: Relational Database Management Systems (RDBMS)
topics.description: RDBMS store data in tables with rows and columns, enforce schemas, support ACID transactions, and use SQL for querying. They are the foundation of most business applications.

interviewQuestions.q: What are the advantages of RDBMS?
interviewQuestions.a: ACID transactions for data integrity, mature and well-understood technology, powerful query language (SQL with JOINs, aggregations, subqueries), strong schema enforcement, rich ecosystem of tools and ORMs, excellent for complex relationships between entities, and decades of optimization research.

interviewQuestions.q: When would you not use an RDBMS?
interviewQuestions.a: When schema changes are very frequent, data is unstructured or semi-structured (logs, documents), you need massive write throughput that exceeds single-server capacity, data does not have clear relationships, you need extreme horizontal scalability, or you are working with graph-like data (social networks).

interviewQuestions.q: Compare PostgreSQL and MySQL.
interviewQuestions.a: PostgreSQL: more feature-rich (JSON support, window functions, CTEs, full-text search), better standard compliance, extensible (custom types, functions), better for complex queries. MySQL: simpler, faster for simple queries, better replication ecosystem, wider hosting support, InnoDB storage engine is reliable. Both are excellent — PostgreSQL is more versatile, MySQL is more straightforward.

tips: PostgreSQL is the best default choice for new projects — it has the richest feature set among open-source RDBMS.
Use connection pooling (PgBouncer, ProxySQL) — database connections are expensive to establish.

topics.title: Key-Value Store
topics.description: Key-value stores provide simple get/set operations with O(1) lookups. They are used for caching, session storage, and simple data models where the access pattern is always by key.

interviewQuestions.q: What are key-value stores and when should you use them?
interviewQuestions.a: Key-value stores map keys to values with O(1) lookups. Use for: caching (Redis, Memcached), session storage, user preferences, shopping carts, rate limiting, leaderboards, and any simple lookup-by-key pattern. They offer extreme performance and scalability but limited query capability — you can only look up by key, not by value attributes.

interviewQuestions.q: Compare Redis and Memcached.
interviewQuestions.a: Redis: supports data structures (strings, lists, sets, hashes, sorted sets), persistence options, pub/sub, Lua scripting, clustering, and replication. Memcached: simpler, multi-threaded (better for simple caching on multi-core), supports only string values, no persistence, simpler horizontal scaling. Choose Redis for feature-rich needs, Memcached for simple, high-throughput caching.

interviewQuestions.q: How does DynamoDB work as a key-value store?
interviewQuestions.a: DynamoDB is a managed key-value and document database. Tables have a partition key (hash) and optional sort key. Single-digit millisecond latency at any scale. Supports secondary indexes for alternative access patterns. Auto-scales throughput. Offers both eventual and strong consistency per-read. Pricing is per read/write capacity unit. Excellent for serverless and high-scale applications.

tips: Redis is the Swiss Army knife of databases — use it for caching, sessions, rate limiting, pub/sub, and queues.
Design your data model around access patterns when using key-value stores — you cannot query by value efficiently.

topics.title: Document Store
topics.description: Document stores organize data as flexible JSON/BSON documents. Each document can have a different structure. They excel at storing semi-structured data and enabling rapid development with evolving schemas.

interviewQuestions.q: What are document stores and when should you use them?
interviewQuestions.a: Document stores store data as JSON-like documents with flexible schemas. Use for: content management, product catalogs, user profiles, event logging, and any domain where entities have varying attributes. Documents can be nested, reducing the need for JOINs. Best when you read/write entire documents and do not need complex cross-document queries.

interviewQuestions.q: What are the limitations of document stores?
interviewQuestions.a: Complex JOINs across documents are expensive or unsupported, less efficient for highly relational data, potential for data duplication (embedded documents), weaker transaction support (though MongoDB now supports multi-document ACID transactions), and query optimization is less mature than RDBMS.

interviewQuestions.q: How does MongoDB handle scaling?
interviewQuestions.a: MongoDB uses sharding (distributing data across servers using a shard key), replica sets (primary + secondaries for HA), and automatic failover. The mongos router directs queries to the correct shard. Config servers store cluster metadata. It supports range-based and hash-based sharding strategies.

tips: Embed related data in a single document when they are always accessed together — this avoids JOINs and improves read performance.
Use references (like foreign keys) when related data is large, frequently updated independently, or accessed separately.

topics.title: Wide Column Store
topics.description: Wide column stores organize data by columns rather than rows. They excel at handling large volumes of data with high write throughput and are ideal for time-series data, IoT, and analytics.

resources.title: Wide Column Store - System Design Primer

interviewQuestions.q: What are wide column stores?
interviewQuestions.a: Wide column stores use tables, rows, and columns, but unlike RDBMS, columns can vary per row and are grouped into column families. Optimized for write-heavy workloads and reading specific columns. Examples: Cassandra, HBase, Google Bigtable, ScyllaDB. Used for time-series data, IoT sensor data, messaging systems, and recommendation engines.

interviewQuestions.q: How does Cassandra achieve high availability?
interviewQuestions.a: Cassandra uses a ring architecture with no single point of failure — all nodes are equal. Data is partitioned across nodes using consistent hashing. Replication factor determines how many copies exist. Tunable consistency (ONE, QUORUM, ALL) lets you balance consistency and availability per operation. Gossip protocol manages cluster membership and failure detection.

interviewQuestions.q: When should you choose a wide column store over RDBMS?
interviewQuestions.a: When you have massive write throughput requirements (millions of writes/second), time-series or event data, data that is naturally columnar, need for linear horizontal scalability, and can work with limited query patterns (primarily key-based lookups). Avoid for complex queries, transactions, or when strong consistency across all operations is required.

tips: Model data in Cassandra around your queries — design tables to serve specific query patterns, even if it means duplicating data.
Wide column stores are ideal for time-series data — partition by time period and sort within partitions.

topics.title: Graph Databases
topics.description: Graph databases store data as nodes and edges (relationships). They excel at traversing relationships and are ideal for social networks, recommendation engines, fraud detection, and knowledge graphs.

interviewQuestions.q: What are graph databases and when should you use them?
interviewQuestions.a: Graph databases model data as nodes (entities), edges (relationships), and properties. Use when: relationships are the core of your data model (social networks, recommendation engines), you need multi-hop traversals (friends of friends, shortest path), the number and type of relationships are dynamic, and relational JOINs become prohibitively expensive for relationship queries.

interviewQuestions.q: How do graph databases differ from relational databases for relationship queries?
interviewQuestions.a: In RDBMS, relationship queries require JOINs that become exponentially expensive with depth (friends-of-friends-of-friends). Graph databases store relationships as first-class citizens — traversing a relationship is O(1) regardless of total data size. A 6-degree connection query that takes minutes in SQL takes milliseconds in a graph database.

interviewQuestions.q: What are the use cases for graph databases?
interviewQuestions.a: Social networks (friend recommendations, mutual connections), fraud detection (finding suspicious transaction patterns), knowledge graphs (Wikipedia, Google Knowledge Graph), network topology (routing, dependency analysis), recommendation engines (users who bought X also bought Y), access control (permission graphs), and supply chain management.

tips: Use graph databases when your most important queries involve traversing relationships — not just storing them.
Graph databases complement rather than replace RDBMS — use both for different parts of your system.

topics.title: NoSQL
topics.description: NoSQL is an umbrella term for non-relational databases. They provide flexible schemas, horizontal scalability, and are optimized for specific data models and access patterns.

interviewQuestions.q: What are the main types of NoSQL databases?
interviewQuestions.a: 1) Key-Value: Redis, DynamoDB — simple lookups by key. 2) Document: MongoDB, CouchDB — flexible JSON documents. 3) Wide Column: Cassandra, HBase — column-family storage for massive data. 4) Graph: Neo4j, Neptune — relationship traversal. Each type is optimized for different access patterns and data models.

interviewQuestions.q: What is BASE and how does it differ from ACID?
interviewQuestions.a: BASE: Basically Available (system always responds), Soft state (state may change without input due to eventual consistency), Eventually consistent (data converges over time). ACID provides strong guarantees at the cost of performance/availability. BASE trades consistency for availability and performance — suitable for systems that can tolerate temporary inconsistency.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you choose the right NoSQL database?
interviewQuestions.a: Match the database to your access pattern: Key-value for simple lookups and caching. Document for flexible schemas and embedded data.
